narrative: On April 24, 2024, I was at my residence in Ahmedabad when I received an unexpected phone call at around 08:42. The caller identified themselves as a senior investigating officer from the ED. The person sounded extremely authoritative and informed me that my Aadhaar card and PAN card details had been found implicated in a major money laundering and contraband smuggling case involving millions of rupees. I am a 39-year-old citizen with no criminal record, and I was completely terrified by this sudden allegation. They immediately escalated the situation by ordering me to isolate myself in an empty room and forcing me to join a Zoom call. Once connected, I saw individuals dressed in official-looking uniforms sitting in a room that closely resembled a real police station, complete with national emblems and official insignia. They declared that I was under 'digital arrest' and strictly prohibited me from muting the microphone, turning off the camera, or contacting any family members or legal counsel. They displayed highly realistic forged documents bearing my name, photograph, and official government stamps, claiming an arrest warrant had already been authorized by the Supreme Court. I was kept under constant surveillance on this harrowing call for over 16 hours without any break. Exploiting my extreme fear, confusion, and panic, the impersonators instructed me to transfer all my personal savings to a 'secret secure ED investigation account' to verify my innocence. They promised the entire amount would be reversed back to my account within 24 hours once my financial audit was clear. Out of sheer panic, I initiated multiple transactions totaling Rs. 2,610,126 via IMPS/NEFT. It was only after the perpetrators abruptly disconnected the call the next evening that I contacted the actual local police station and realized I had been the victim of an elaborate and devastating psychological scam.

I am filing this formal complaint regarding a highly sophisticated cyber fraud that occurred on July 21, 2024. I am a 34-year-old resident of Delhi. I received an automated Interactive Voice Response (IVR) call claiming that a mobile number registered in my name was being used to send illegal threat messages and that my cellular services would be blocked in two hours. I pressed '9' to speak to an executive and was connected to a man claiming to represent ED. He transferred my call to what he described as the 'cyber crime investigation branch'. I was then manipulated into logging on to a Zoom call for formal questioning. The individuals on the screen were wearing uniforms and sitting behind heavy wooden desks with the ED logo prominently displayed in the background. They informed me that multiple bank accounts had been opened using my PAN card to fund illicit activities across international borders. They convinced me that I was a prime suspect and placed me under a so-called 'digital arrest'. They claimed that my local physical movements were currently being tracked and any attempt to log off would result in immediate physical detention by a local raid team. Under incredible mental distress and duress spanning 7 hours, they convinced me that the only way to avoid formal imprisonment was to deposit a 'security clearance bond'. Desperate to defend my reputation and believing their threats were completely legitimate, I gathered funds by liquidating my fixed deposits and transferred Rs. 3,118,343 using RTGS to the accounts they dictated. The scammers assured me the money was just held in an escrow account. I am left financially ruined and deeply traumatized by this impersonation scam.

My nightmare began on the morning of July 11, 2024 in Lucknow. I received an urgent call from someone pretending to be a customs official at the international airport. They claimed that a parcel addressed to me contained illegal narcotics and multiple expired passports. When I denied any knowledge of the parcel, they stated the matter was being escalated to the CBI and forced me to immediately download an application to initiate a Skype call. I am 58 years old and not very tech-savvy, so I blindly followed their instructions out of fear of legal trouble. Upon joining the call, a person dressed as a senior CBI director interrogated me aggressively. They digitally projected a fake FIR document onto the screen, which had my exact residential address and identity details. They told me I could not leave the camera frame and declared me under 'digital arrest'. I was held hostage virtually for 40 straight hours. Whenever I requested to drink water or use the restroom, they threatened to send local constables to publicly arrest me in front of my neighbors. They convinced me to temporarily transfer Rs. 1,149,319 to an 'auditor verification ledger' via RTGS to prove that my funds were legally acquired and not linked to the intercepted parcel. Believing my life and career were on the line, I complied with all their demands. After the initial transfer, they demanded further payments, at which point I became suspicious and disconnected. I immediately checked with my bank, but the funds had already been moved.

I am filing this formal complaint regarding a highly sophisticated cyber fraud that occurred on February 01, 2024. I am a 37-year-old resident of Bangalore. I received an automated Interactive Voice Response (IVR) call claiming that a mobile number registered in my name was being used to send illegal threat messages and that my cellular services would be blocked in two hours. I pressed '9' to speak to an executive and was connected to a man claiming to represent TRAI. He transferred my call to what he described as the 'cyber crime investigation branch'. I was then manipulated into logging on to a WhatsApp video call for formal questioning. The individuals on the screen were wearing uniforms and sitting behind heavy wooden desks with the TRAI logo prominently displayed in the background. They informed me that multiple bank accounts had been opened using my PAN card to fund illicit activities across international borders. They convinced me that I was a prime suspect and placed me under a so-called 'digital arrest'. They claimed that my local physical movements were currently being tracked and any attempt to log off would result in immediate physical detention by a local raid team. Under incredible mental distress and duress spanning 36 hours, they convinced me that the only way to avoid formal imprisonment was to deposit a 'security clearance bond'. Desperate to defend my reputation and believing their threats were completely legitimate, I gathered funds by liquidating my fixed deposits and transferred Rs. 4,908,516 using UPI to the accounts they dictated. The scammers assured me the money was just held in an escrow account. I am left financially ruined and deeply traumatized by this impersonation scam.

On September 17, 2024, I was at my residence in Kolkata when I received an unexpected phone call at around 14:43. The caller identified themselves as a senior investigating officer from the TRAI. The person sounded extremely authoritative and informed me that my Aadhaar card and PAN card details had been found implicated in a major money laundering and contraband smuggling case involving millions of rupees. I am a 46-year-old citizen with no criminal record, and I was completely terrified by this sudden allegation. They immediately escalated the situation by ordering me to isolate myself in an empty room and forcing me to join a video call. Once connected, I saw individuals dressed in official-looking uniforms sitting in a room that closely resembled a real police station, complete with national emblems and official insignia. They declared that I was under 'digital arrest' and strictly prohibited me from muting the microphone, turning off the camera, or contacting any family members or legal counsel. They displayed highly realistic forged documents bearing my name, photograph, and official government stamps, claiming an arrest warrant had already been authorized by the Supreme Court. I was kept under constant surveillance on this harrowing call for over 43 hours without any break. Exploiting my extreme fear, confusion, and panic, the impersonators instructed me to transfer all my personal savings to a 'secret secure TRAI investigation account' to verify my innocence. They promised the entire amount would be reversed back to my account within 24 hours once my financial audit was clear. Out of sheer panic, I initiated multiple transactions totaling Rs. 2,995,627 via IMPS/NEFT. It was only after the perpetrators abruptly disconnected the call the next evening that I contacted the actual local police station and realized I had been the victim of an elaborate and devastating psychological scam.

My nightmare began on the morning of September 14, 2024 in Lucknow. I received an urgent call from someone pretending to be a customs official at the international airport. I immediately checked with my bank, but the funds had already been moved.

Upon joining the call, a person dressed as a senior Customs director interrogated me aggressively. They digitally projected a fake FIR document onto the screen, which had my exact residential address and identity details. They told me I could not leave the camera frame and declared me under 'digital arrest'. I was held hostage virtually for 48 straight hours. Whenever I requested to drink water or use the restroom, they threatened to send local constables to publicly arrest me in front of my neighbors. They convinced me to temporarily transfer Rs. 2,995,238 to an 'auditor verification ledger' via IMPS/NEFT to prove that my funds were legally acquired and not linked to the intercepted parcel. Believing my life and career were on the line, I complied with all their demands. After the initial transfer, they demanded further payments, at which point I became suspicious and disconnected. I immediately checked with my bank, but the funds had already been moved.

On October 03, 2024, I was at my residence in Ahmedabad when I received an unexpected phone call at around 20:46. The caller identified themselves as a senior investigating officer from the ED. I was kept under constant surveillance on this harrowing call for over 15 hours without any break. Out of sheer panic, I initiated multiple transactions totaling Rs. 3,599,538 via RTGS. It was only after the perpetrators abruptly disconnected the call the next evening that I contacted the actual local police station and realized I had been the victim of an elaborate and devastating psychological scam.

My nightmare began on the morning of August 19, 2024 in Hyderabad. I received an urgent call from someone pretending to be a customs official at the international airport. They claimed that a parcel addressed to me contained illegal narcotics and multiple expired passports. When I denied any knowledge of the parcel, they stated the matter was being escalated to the TRAI and forced me to immediately download an application to initiate a Zoom call. I am 70 years old and not very tech-savvy, so I blindly followed their instructions out of fear of legal trouble. Upon joining the call, a person dressed as a senior TRAI director interrogated me aggressively. They digitally projected a fake FIR document onto the screen, which had my exact residential address and identity details. They told me I could not leave the camera frame and declared me under 'digital arrest'. I was held hostage virtually for 35 straight hours. Whenever I requested to drink water or use the restroom, they threatened to send local constables to publicly arrest me in front of my neighbors. They convinced me to temporarily transfer Rs. 3,725,049 to an 'auditor verification ledger' via IMPS/NEFT to prove that my funds were legally acquired and not linked to the intercepted parcel. Believing my life and career were on the line, I complied with all their demands. After the initial transfer, they demanded further payments, at which point I became suspicious and disconnected. I immediately checked with my bank, but the funds had already been moved.

I downloaded a quick loan app from the Play Store to cover a medical emergency in Delhi. I applied for a 5,000 rupee loan but they only disbursed 3,000, claiming the rest was processing fees. A week later, they demanded Rs. 296,326 and threatened to send morphed obscene photos of me to all my contacts using the contact list permission I had originally granted. The harassment was unbearable.

On August 14, 2024, I was at my residence in Pune when I received an unexpected phone call at around 12:11. The caller identified themselves as a senior investigating officer from the CBI. The person sounded extremely authoritative and informed me that my Aadhaar card and PAN card details had been found implicated in a major money laundering and contraband smuggling case involving millions of rupees. I am a 61-year-old citizen with no criminal record, and I was completely terrified by this sudden allegation. They immediately escalated the situation by ordering me to isolate myself in an empty room and forcing me to join a WhatsApp video call. Once connected, I saw individuals dressed in official-looking uniforms sitting in a room that closely resembled a real police station, complete with national emblems and official insignia. They declared that I was under 'digital arrest' and strictly prohibited me from muting the microphone, turning off the camera, or contacting any family members or legal counsel. They displayed highly realistic forged documents bearing my name, photograph, and official government stamps, claiming an arrest warrant had already been authorized by the Supreme Court. I was kept under constant surveillance on this harrowing call for over 43 hours without any break. Exploiting my extreme fear, confusion, and panic, the impersonators instructed me to transfer all my personal savings to a 'secret secure CBI investigation account' to verify my innocence. They promised the entire amount would be reversed back to my account within 24 hours once my financial audit was clear. Out of sheer panic, I initiated multiple transactions totaling Rs. 4,403,384 via crypto exchange transfer. It was only after the perpetrators abruptly disconnected the call the next evening that I contacted the actual local police station and realized I had been the victim of an elaborate and devastating psychological scam.

I scanned and authorized Rs. 32,018, but soon realized the cash he handed me were counterfeit notes. I am a 61-year-old resident of Pune.

I received an SMS stating that my electricity bill for my Lucknow residence was unpaid and power would be disconnected tonight. The message contained a payment link. I clicked it and was redirected to a payment gateway that looked legitimate. I entered my UPI details, and Rs. 268,236 was withdrawn automatically. The sender's number is now switched off.

On May 18, 2024, I was trying to sell some old furniture online. A buyer contacted me and agreed to the price. However, instead of sending money, they sent a QR code and a link via WhatsApp. I clicked the link, and it asked for my UPI PIN. Thinking it was required to receive the payment, I entered my PIN. Instantly, Rs. 283,369 was deducted from my account. I live in Hyderabad and I'm 72 years old.

My nightmare began on the morning of July 30, 2024 in Kolkata. I received an urgent call from someone pretending to be a customs official at the international airport. They claimed that a parcel addressed to me contained illegal narcotics and multiple expired passports. When I denied any knowledge of the parcel, they stated the matter was being escalated to the Police and forced me to immediately download an application to initiate a WhatsApp video call. I am 64 years old and not very tech-savvy, so I blindly followed their instructions out of fear of legal trouble. Upon joining the call, a person dressed as a senior Police director interrogated me aggressively. They digitally projected a fake FIR document onto the screen, which had my exact residential address and identity details. They told me I could not leave the camera frame and declared me under 'digital arrest'. I was held hostage virtually for 13 straight hours. Whenever I requested to drink water or use the restroom, they threatened to send local constables to publicly arrest me in front of my neighbors. They convinced me to temporarily transfer Rs. 1,413,163 to an 'auditor verification ledger' via bank transfer to prove that my funds were legally acquired and not linked to the intercepted parcel. Believing my life and career were on the line, I complied with all their demands. After the initial transfer, they demanded further payments, at which point I became suspicious and disconnected. I immediately checked with my bank, but the funds had already been moved.

I was facing issues with my broadband connection in Mumbai. I googled the customer care number and called the first result. The person on the other end asked me to install a remote access app called AnyDesk to fix the router settings. Within minutes of granting access, they opened my banking app and transferred Rs. 198,744 out of my account. I am 37 years old.
